lines.text: Against Me!, 'Sink, Florida, Sink.' New Wave, 2007. Laura Jane Grace wrote this one thinking about home — Naples, then Gainesville. Both sinking into the swamp in different ways.
Grace moved to Gainesville in '97, just a kid with a guitar and a feeling that the whole world was against her. That's where the band name came from — 'The whole world is against me.' Literal. True.
James Bowman on guitar since 2001. Solid. Consistent. The kind of guy who shows up. This song's got that electric punch — they'd moved from folk demos to full band by then. Evolution.

New Wave hit number 57 on the Billboard. Sire Records. They were on the map. And they're singing about their state drowning. Metaphor or prophecy? {{CLOCK}} Madison time, and I'm asking the real questions.
Grace built Total Treble Studio in Elkton, Florida in 2011. Her own space. Control. That's punk rock in 2169 — you can't trust anyone else's machines.
The Lawrence Arms, 'Coming Home.' 2003. The Greatest Story Ever Told album. These guys are from Chicago. They know about coming home to nothing.

Brendan Kelly, Chris McCaughan, Neil Hennessy. They formed in '99, took their name from an apartment complex they got evicted from — middle of the night. That's the Chicago origin story. That's the punk story.

Kelly was in Slapstick, McCaughan was in the Broadways. They brought that ska energy, that ska *humor*, into something meaner. Dark humor. Personal mythology. Fat Mike signed them to Fat Wreck in 2001.
Greatest Story Ever Told had footnotes. Actual footnotes. History, literature, pop culture woven into every lyric. These weren't songs — they were *arguments*. They were essays sung in three minutes.
Kelly said, 'We all have experiences — good ones and terrible ones — influenced by alcohol.' That's not a quote from an interview. That's confession. That's the whole album right there.
Coming home to Madison tonight. Clock reads {{CLOCK}}. Some of you are nine hours into this. Some of you are thinking about opening the door. Don't. Stay with me. Stay with the Lawrence Arms.

tracks.title: A Good Drink, Good People, Good Fun

lines.text: Hot Water Music, 'A Good Drink, Good People, Good Fun.' Fuel for the Hate Game, 1997. These guys took their name from a Bukowski collection. Which means they read literature and drank whiskey and wrote punk songs.
Chuck Ragan, Chris Wollard, Jason Black, George Rebelo. Gainesville. Four guys who moved there because Black saw the music scene and said, 'That's it. Come here.' They did.
Dual vocals. Call-and-response. Gritty, overlapping shouts. You hear one guy, then the other cuts him off, and suddenly you're hearing two truths at once. Community. Camaraderie. That's their thing.

The band broke up in '98 because touring exhausted them. They were killing their friendships. Then they reconvened for a farewell show and realized mid-rehearsal they didn't want to break up. So they didn't. Love does that.

They've split up twice since then. They're back now. Resilience. Working-class identity. Emotional vulnerability. That's the whole Gainesville school right there — Grace, Hot Water Music, the whole scene. Tough people singing soft songs.

Bouncing Souls, 'Ole.' 1997. New Brunswick, New Jersey. Greg Attonito, Pete Steinkopf, Bryan Kienlen, George Rebelo. Four kids from Basking Ridge who moved to a college town because that's where the punk scene was.
Steinkopf said, 'We were just a band that played our friends' backyards.' That's the entire history of punk rock right there. Not venues. Not records. Backyards. Your friends. Your neighborhood.

The band name came from Doc Martens advertising. 'Bouncing Soles.' They saw an ad and went, 'That's us.' That's the kind of dumb genius punk rock requires. Also, they made 'Candy' and 'What Boys Like' as a jab at homophobia. Dark humor, serious intent.
Epitaph Records signed them in '97 after Youth Brigade noticed them. Their melodic punk reignited the 'pogo' element to Jersey punk. Fast, light-hearted, singalong. You can't help yourself.
'I like your mom and it's no fad, I wanna marry her and be your dad.' That's the opening line of one of their songs. *That* is punk rock. Silly and sincere. No difference.
Thirty years running. Thirty years of hometown pride and backyard philosophy. We're nine hours in, folks. You need a sing-along. You need 'Ole.' You need to remember why you stayed home.

tracks.title: Sunday Morning
tracks.artist: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes

lines.text: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes, 'Sunday Morning.' 1998. This is a cover band. Spike Slawson, C.J. Ramone, Joey Cape, Pinch, John Reis. Superstars of their own bands, playing other people's songs for fun.

They formed in San Francisco in '95 during off-time from NOFX, Foo Fighters, Lagwagon. Fat Mike, Joey Cape, Chris Shiflett — guys who had *real* bands — decided to just cover songs and see what happened.

They didn't plan to release albums. But they did. Have a Ball, 1997 — pop hits from the '60s and '70s. Then they did Broadway show tunes, country, R&B. Every album a different genre. Every album punk as hell.

The liner notes from one live album describe them playing a Bar Mitzvah for thirty people and making a lot of mistakes. Starting songs, stopping, starting again. That's the freedom. That's the whole point.

They wear matching costumes. Weird ones. For Love Their Country, cowboy outfits. For Are a Drag, they dressed as characters from musicals. Punk rock as theater. Humor as weapon. Three hours until sunrise. You need softness. You need this.

NOFX, 'The Decline' — acoustic version, 2010. Fat Mike Burkett, Erik Sandin, Eric Melvin, El Hefe. Formed in 1983 at Fairfax High School in Los Angeles. They have never signed to a major label. Never.

The original 'Decline' was eighteen minutes of single-track extended play. Pure cynical social commentary. Fiery. Angry. In 2010, after twenty-seven years of punk, they made it acoustic. Stripping it down. Making you *listen*.
Punk in Drublic, 1994. Gold status six years later. Fat Mike said about the 'Leave It Alone' video, 'We didn't want to be part of that machine, that punk wave.' They meant it. They still mean it.

They made an album called The War on Errorism in 2003. Political. Anti-Bush. But they were always political — humor as cover for rage. Racism, middle-class plight, drug abuse. They wrote 'Six Years on Dope' detailing everything it cost.

The acoustic version of 'The Decline' is them old. Tired. Still angry. But softer. You can hear Fat Mike's voice clearer — no screaming, no irony, just the words. The decline is real. It's still happening. {{CLOCK}} Madison. Still happening.
NOFX started on an independent label. They own Fat Wreck Chords now. They signed everyone else. They're still here. That's the joke. That's the victory. That's why we're still broadcasting.

Descendents, 'Hope.' Milo Goes to College, 1982. Frank Navetta, Tony Lombardo, Bill Stevenson, and Milo Aukerman. Manhattan Beach, California. They were power-pop surf punks who became melodic hardcore.
Milo Aukerman sang about things that *mattered* to young punks. Parents. Food. Girls. Being rejected. That's the entire human experience right there. Not epics. Not mythology. Homework and sadness.

Milo Goes to College reconciled the antisocial fury of Black Flag with diary-entry vulnerability and sing-along hooks. You could scream and cry at the same time. You *should* scream and cry at the same time.
Bill Stevenson was recording engineer. He also engineered punk records. He *is* punk. Later he built The Blasting Room in Fort Collins. He controls the space now. Control is everything.
Stevenson's boat was named Orca. He and a friend invented a concept on that boat called 'All' — doing the utmost, achieving the utmost, turning it into a religion. That became their whole philosophy.

Milo became a biochemist at DuPont. Punk rock and chemistry. Stevenson said of the original members, 'They saved me from being a lonely, miserable little boy.' That's what this music does. It saves you. Hope. The last thing in Pandora's box. We need it {{ELAPSED}} in.

tickerItems.text: Madison-wide: 165 confirmed at this point last year. Current pace tracking +8% YoY. East Side holding steady at 34% of total activity.
Helicopter traffic overhead unconfirmed origin. Government? Private security? Tim reports signal clean. Stay tuned.
Sunrise in 2 hours 47 minutes. Pre-dawn activity historically spikes between 05:00–06:00. Barricade integrity checks recommended NOW.
